refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies a specific DP) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent designated work zones (operational areas, restricted zones, safety perimeters) in the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and retrieve worksite spatial data in a format accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The candidate FR is not atomic; it has been decomposed into four atomic sub-FRs, each addressing a single functional obligation without internal 'and'/'or' logic.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature inventory per zone via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; represent as a hierarchical zone-feature registry with versioning and access control.

DP3: Establish a real-time spatial streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and conflict resolution, and publishes a continuously-updated feature map accessible via standardized query interface.

DP4: Capture worksite features as a rule-based declarative model (constraints, exclusion zones, operational corridors) derived from safety plan and policies; generate executable spatial representations on-demand for planning/execution consumers.

DP5: Implement a federated feature model where each subsystem (e.g., safety monitoring, path planning, equipment tracking) maintains its own feature abstraction; synchronize via a central schema registry and conflict resolution protocol.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

potential_sources: Sensor fusion logic tightly coupled to specific sensor modalities—mitigated by abstracting sensor interface layer and fusion algorithm as independent DP sub-components.
Coordinate frame definition and transformation dependencies—mitigated by establishing a canonical worksite frame early and documenting all transformations.
Feature classification schema coupling to downstream planning/execution—mitigated by versioning the schema and providing backward-compatible query interfaces.
Real-time update latency requirements may couple storage format to query performance—mitigated by separating logical model (spatial graph) from physical storage/indexing strategy.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits moderate coupling risk primarily at the sensor-fusion and schema-definition boundaries. Coupling is manageable through clear interface contracts and separation of concerns (acquisition → fusion → storage → query). DP1 avoids hard dependencies on specific technologies and allows substitution of sensing modalities or fusion algorithms without restructuring the core model.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) is available and can be synchronized. Risk: If only single-modality sensing is available, fusion logic becomes trivial; if sensors have significant latency skew, synchronization becomes a critical DP sub-problem.

Assumption: Worksite features are stationary or change slowly relative to sensing cadence. Risk: Highly dynamic environments (e.g., moving equipment, temporary barriers) may require continuous re-acquisition and conflict resolution; model staleness could violate safety constraints.

Assumption: Planning/execution functions can tolerate a bounded latency between feature acquisition and availability. Risk: If real-time responsiveness is critical (e.g., collision avoidance), the query interface and update frequency must be explicitly constrained in DP refinement.
Assumption: Feature classification taxonomy is well-defined and stable. Risk: If safety plan or operational policies evolve, the schema must be versioned and migration strategy established.

Assumption: Worksite coordinate frame is stable and can be established once. Risk: If the frame drifts (e.g., due to sensor calibration drift or site subsidence), periodic re-calibration and model reconciliation are required.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria 'agreed set of site features' is pre-defined. Risk: If feature scope is ambiguous or evolves, the model may become incomplete or inconsistent; explicit feature inventory and change control process required.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP1 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) DP1a: Establish and maintain a canonical worksite coordinate frame with periodic calibration checkpoints. (2) DP1b: Acquire raw sensor observations and synchronize across modalities using a time-alignment protocol. (3) DP1c: Fuse synchronized observations into a feature-attributed spatial graph using a conflict-resolution strategy aligned with safety priorities. (4) DP1d: Expose the spatial graph via a versioned, queryable interface with latency and consistency guarantees. This decomposition clarifies dependencies, enables independent testing of each sub-DP, and supports incremental deployment. Additionally, establish explicit traceability from worksite safety plan and operational policies to the feature classification schema to ensure model completeness and safety compliance.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Onboard spatial sensing, feature fusion, and structured spatial graph storage with query interfaces for planning and execution subsystems

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle exports fused spatial graph state, feature attributes, and safety zone status to external monitoring/logging

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives planning/execution queries against the spatial graph (e.g., 'retrieve obstacles in region X', 'classify features by safety zone')

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle emits alerts when spatial features violate safety constraints or when sensor fusion confidence drops below threshold

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle may receive supplementary sensor calibration or map priors from remote NGHE Solution; transmits spatial graph summaries for teleoperation situational awareness

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: multi-modal sensing hardware (vision, range, inertial) is physically integrated on vehicle; fusion logic and spatial graph storage are naturally onboard. Minor diffusion: safety zone interpretation may require coordination with Safety Services for policy updates; teleoperation may request real-time graph snapshots via Network Link.

Poor fit: Safety Services is a policy/monitoring layer, not a sensor integration layer. Spatial feature acquisition and fusion are fundamentally vehicle-resident. High diffusion: would require Vehicle to stream raw sensor data to Safety Services for fusion, then receive fused graph back—introduces latency, redundancy, and tight coupling. Violates separation of concerns.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a remote human-in-the-loop interface, not an onboard sensing/fusion layer. Spatial graph must be acquired and maintained onboard for real-time planning/execution. High diffusion: would require all sensor data to be transmitted to remote operator, fused remotely, then sent back—introduces unacceptable latency and network dependency for safety-critical spatial awareness.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is a system-level orchestration/coordination layer, not a sensor integration layer. Spatial graph is vehicle-specific and must be maintained onboard. Moderate-to-high diffusion: NGHE Solution would need to aggregate spatial graphs from multiple vehicles, manage fusion policies, and distribute queries—adds unnecessary indirection and coupling. Better as a consumer of Vehicle-generated spatial graph via Telemetry/Tasking.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural and optimal boundary for DP1 because: (1) all sensing hardware (vision, range, inertial) is physically integrated on the vehicle; (2) fusion logic must execute onboard for real-time latency and autonomy; (3) the spatial graph is a vehicle-local state artifact queried by onboard planning/execution; (4) external consumers (Safety Services, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) interact with the graph via well-defined interfaces (Tasking for queries, Telemetry for state export, SafetyAlert for exceptions) rather than owning the fusion process.
containment_risks: Risk: If vehicle computational resources are severely constrained, fusion logic may need to be offloaded to NGHE Solution or edge compute. Mitigation: design fusion DP with pluggable backends (onboard vs. remote) and explicit latency/bandwidth contracts via Network Link interface.

Risk: Safety zone interpretation and feature classification policies may evolve independently of vehicle firmware. Mitigation: externalize policy as queryable configuration via Tasking interface; Safety Services owns policy, Vehicle owns execution.

Risk: Multi-vehicle scenarios may require cross-vehicle spatial graph merging or consensus. Mitigation: Vehicle maintains local graph; NGHE Solution coordinates inter-vehicle fusion via Telemetry aggregation and Tasking broadcast.

Risk: Teleoperation may demand real-time spatial graph streaming for situational awareness. Mitigation: define Telemetry export schema and sampling rate; use Network Link for bandwidth-constrained transmission.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model/map generation and availability to planning/execution functions can be tested via Tasking interface queries; (2) feature set coverage (geometry, classification, safety zone) can be validated against agreed site features list. The Vehicle IM boundary provides a clear, testable artifact (spatial graph) with defined query semantics.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The Vehicle module is unambiguously the owner of DP1 realization. All sensing hardware is physically integrated; fusion logic executes onboard; spatial graph is a vehicle-local state artifact. External consumers (Safety Services, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) interact via well-defined, asynchronous interfaces (Tasking queries, Telemetry exports, SafetyAlert notifications) rather than owning or co-owning the fusion process. Ownership boundaries are crisp and non-overlapping.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 is logically sound and complete for FR. It specifies: (1) multi-modal sensing acquisition (vision, range, inertial)—addresses 'capture'; (2) fusion into unified coordinate frame—addresses 'represent'; (3) structured spatial graph with queryable attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone)—addresses 'machine-readable model' and 'available to planning/execution'. DP1 is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific algorithms or hardware SKUs) and maps cleanly to the FR without logical gaps or over-specification.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Safety zone interpretation and feature classification policies are externalized to Safety Services (via Tasking interface and configuration updates). If policy evolution frequency is high or policy-to-execution latency is critical, Vehicle may need to cache policies and handle stale-policy scenarios. Mitigation: define policy versioning and cache invalidation semantics in Tasking interface contract.

Moderate risk: Teleoperation may demand real-time spatial graph streaming at high frequency/fidelity for situational awareness. Network Link bandwidth constraints could force lossy compression or sampling, degrading operator situational awareness. Mitigation: define Telemetry export schema with configurable sampling rates and feature filtering; establish explicit latency/bandwidth SLAs.

Low-to-moderate risk: Multi-vehicle spatial graph merging or consensus (if required by future operational scenarios) is not explicitly addressed in current DP1. NGHE Solution would need to aggregate Vehicle-exported graphs via Telemetry, but fusion policy for cross-vehicle conflicts is undefined. Mitigation: defer to future FR/DP if multi-vehicle fusion is required; current DP1 is single-vehicle scoped.

Low risk: Vehicle computational resource constraints could force offloading of fusion logic to NGHE Solution or edge compute. This would violate the onboard latency/autonomy requirement. Mitigation: design DP1 with pluggable fusion backends (onboard vs. remote) and explicit latency contracts; validate vehicle compute budget during design review.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. Vehicle module scores 0.92 containment / 0.08 diffusion. All core DP1 responsibilities (multi-modal sensing acquisition, fusion, spatial graph storage) are naturally and physically integrated on the vehicle. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and asynchronous, avoiding tight coupling or responsibility diffusion. The four alternative module candidates (Safety Services, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) all score poorly (0.25–0.40 containment) because they are policy/orchestration/interface layers, not sensor integration layers. No evidence of DP1 being scattered across multiple module boundaries or responsibilities. The minor diffusion noted (safety zone policy coordination, teleoperation graph streaming) is managed via explicit interface contracts and does not compromise Vehicle ownership of the core fusion logic.

recommended_revisions: Clarify DP1 to explicitly state that fusion logic executes onboard the Vehicle for real-time latency and autonomy, with optional remote fusion backends available via Network Link for resource-constrained scenarios. Add latency/bandwidth SLA requirements to the DP.

Define Tasking interface contract to include: (a) query semantics for spatial graph (e.g., 'retrieve obstacles in region X', 'classify features by safety zone'); (b) policy update mechanism for safety zone definitions and feature classification rules; (c) versioning and cache invalidation semantics to handle stale policies.

Define Telemetry export schema to include: (a) spatial graph state (geometry, classification, safety zone attributes); (b) fusion confidence metrics per feature; (c) sensor health/calibration status; (d) configurable sampling rates and feature filtering for bandwidth-constrained scenarios (e.g., teleoperation).
Add explicit acceptance criterion: 'Spatial graph is queryable by planning/execution functions with latency ≤ [X] ms and availability ≥ [Y]%' to make verification testable and quantifiable.
Document multi-vehicle spatial graph merging as a future FR/DP if cross-vehicle fusion is required; current DP1 is single-vehicle scoped and does not address consensus or conflict resolution.

Validate Vehicle computational budget during design review to confirm onboard fusion is feasible; if not, trigger design alternative (remote fusion backend) and re-evaluate DP1 latency/autonomy requirements.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is clear, verifiable, and well-scoped to the Vehicle module. DP1 is logically sound and complete. However, the gate results surface four actionable diffusion risks and six recommended revisions that must be resolved before decomposition proceeds. Rather than stop prematurely (which would leave critical interface contracts and resource constraints unvalidated) or revise at the current level (which would delay decomposition), the optimal path is to decompose DP1 into sub-level FRs that explicitly address the identified risks: (1) onboard fusion latency/autonomy guarantees, (2) Tasking interface contract (query semantics, policy versioning), (3) Telemetry export schema (sampling, filtering, confidence metrics), and (4) computational resource feasibility. This approach converts risks into explicit functional requirements, making them traceable, verifiable, and owned at the appropriate decomposition level. The high safety criticality of the system reinforces the need to decompose and validate these concerns rather than defer them.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Execute multi-modal sensor fusion logic onboard the Vehicle with latency ≤ [X] ms and availability ≥ [Y]% to ensure real-time autonomy and independence from network connectivity.

FR1.2: Provide queryable spatial graph interface (Tasking) that supports region-based obstacle retrieval, feature classification by safety zone, and policy-driven attribute updates with versioning and cache invalidation semantics.

FR1.3: Export spatial graph state and fusion metadata (geometry, classification, safety zone, confidence metrics, sensor health) via Telemetry with configurable sampling rates and feature filtering to support bandwidth-constrained teleoperation and remote monitoring.

FR1.4: Validate and allocate Vehicle computational budget to confirm onboard fusion feasibility; if infeasible, support pluggable remote fusion backends via Network Link with explicit latency/autonomy trade-off documentation.

FR1.5: Define multi-vehicle spatial graph merging and consensus policy (deferred to future FR if cross-vehicle fusion is operationally required); current single-vehicle scope must be explicitly documented and validated against operational scenarios.

revise.why: DP1 is logically sound but lacks explicit specification of latency/autonomy guarantees, interface contracts (Tasking query semantics, policy versioning, Telemetry schema), and computational resource constraints. Revise DP1 to incorporate: (a) onboard fusion execution with optional remote backends and explicit latency SLAs; (b) Tasking interface contract including query semantics, policy update mechanism, and cache invalidation; (c) Telemetry export schema with sampling/filtering and confidence metrics; (d) computational budget validation and fallback strategies. These revisions convert gate-identified risks into explicit design parameters, making them verifiable and traceable through decomposition.

assumptions: Onboard fusion latency requirement [X] ms and availability [Y]% are defined by operational safety and autonomy constraints; these will be derived from FR1.1 decomposition.
Tasking interface is the primary mechanism for external consumers (Safety Services, Planning) to query spatial graph; this assumption is validated by FR1.2.
Vehicle computational budget is sufficient for onboard fusion; if not, remote fusion backend is acceptable with documented latency/autonomy trade-offs (FR1.4).
Multi-vehicle spatial graph merging is not required for current operational scope; if future scenarios demand it, FR1.5 will be activated.

Safety zone interpretation and feature classification policies are externalized to Safety Services and updated via Tasking interface; Vehicle caches policies with versioning and invalidation semantics (FR1.2).
Teleoperation bandwidth constraints are known and will drive Telemetry sampling/filtering strategy (FR1.3).
open_risks: Moderate: Onboard fusion latency may exceed autonomy requirements if Vehicle compute is resource-constrained. Mitigation: FR1.4 validates budget; if infeasible, remote fusion backend is triggered with explicit latency/autonomy trade-off analysis.

Moderate: Tasking interface policy update frequency and cache invalidation latency could cause stale-policy execution if not carefully managed. Mitigation: FR1.2 defines versioning and invalidation semantics; design review validates policy-to-execution latency.

Moderate: Telemetry export bandwidth constraints could degrade teleoperation situational awareness if sampling/filtering is too aggressive. Mitigation: FR1.3 defines configurable sampling rates and explicit latency/bandwidth SLAs; operational testing validates operator situational awareness.

Low-to-moderate: Multi-vehicle spatial graph merging is deferred; if future scenarios require it, consensus policy and conflict resolution are undefined. Mitigation: FR1.5 documents single-vehicle scope; future FR/DP will address multi-vehicle fusion if needed.

Low: Vehicle computational resource constraints could force offloading of fusion logic, violating onboard latency/autonomy requirement. Mitigation: FR1.4 validates compute budget and defines pluggable fusion backends with explicit trade-offs.
evidence_needed: Operational latency and availability requirements for spatial graph (to define [X] ms and [Y]% in FR1.1).
Tasking interface query semantics and policy update frequency (to validate FR1.2 contract design).
Teleoperation bandwidth constraints and operator situational awareness requirements (to validate FR1.3 sampling/filtering strategy).
Vehicle computational budget and fusion algorithm complexity estimates (to validate FR1.4 feasibility).
Operational scenarios requiring multi-vehicle spatial graph merging (to determine if FR1.5 is activated or deferred).
Safety-critical acceptance criteria for spatial graph accuracy, latency, and availability (to support high safety criticality tailoring).
Network Link latency and bandwidth SLAs (to validate remote fusion backend feasibility in FR1.4).
